explicit_text: I was drafted into the Seabees during World War II after asking specifically for the Navy. I ended up in the 18th Special Battalion, which was focused on loading ships. We trained in Williamsburg, Virginia, and Providence, Rhode Island, before heading overseas. Being part of the Seabees meant I was involved in essential logistics and support roles, even though I initially thought I would serve as a machinist.

explicit_text: Before I was drafted, I worked at Teletype Corporation for many years, about forty-six in total. It was a government company that made communications equipment for the military. Because I was a machinist in the machine shop, I received three deferments from the draft while working there. This job was important and directly connected to the war effort even before I joined the Navy.

explicit_text: My first training with the Seabees was at a camp in Williamsburg, Virginia. We spent about two months there learning the basics before moving on to more hands-on training. It was the starting point of my Navy service during World War II.

explicit_text: After Williamsburg, we went to Providence, Rhode Island, for advanced training on ship loading techniques. We practiced using canned goods and cigarettes to simulate cargo. This six-week training prepared us for the real tasks we would face overseas.

explicit_text: We returned to Norfolk, Virginia, after training in Providence to board the USS Knox. Norfolk was the last stop before we headed out through the Panama Canal to the Pacific. It was a major naval base and a key point in our deployment.

explicit_text: I boarded the USS Knox in Norfolk, Virginia, with about 12,000 other men. The Knox was our transport ship as we traveled through the Panama Canal and into the Pacific. It was a large destroyer escort, and we experienced rough seas and seasickness on the journey.

explicit_text: Passing through the Panama Canal was a memorable part of our journey. We had a special pilot from the Panama Canal staff to guide us, but there was an incident where the pilot got too close and wrecked one of our lifeboats. We had to stay overnight because the lifeboats needed repair before continuing into the Pacific.

explicit_text: We arrived in Hawaii after crossing the Pacific, and I remember it was on April 23rd, which was my grandfather's birthday and Shakespeare's birthday too. We stayed there for about five weeks, training and waiting for further orders. It was a chance to experience the islands and even go on liberty to Honolulu.

explicit_text: I saw Diamond Head in Hawaii on my grandfather's birthday, April 23rd. It was a memorable moment for me, especially since I later learned my grandfather had passed away. Diamond Head was a striking landmark during our stay in Hawaii.

explicit_text: While in Hawaii, we were allowed to go on liberty to Honolulu. Getting there was a challenge since we had to hitch rides on jeeps or with other guys. Honolulu had shops for souvenirs, and I even went swimming at Waikiki Beach nearby, though the coral was rough on my feet.

explicit_text: I went swimming at Waikiki Beach during my liberty in Hawaii. The coral there was sharp and tore up my feet, which made it a tough experience. Still, it was a memorable part of my time in Honolulu during the war.

explicit_text: Our ship was stationed near Enewetak Atoll, where atomic bomb tests had been conducted. We didn't go ashore, but we stayed on a merchant ship nearby. It was a strange feeling being so close to such a significant and dangerous site during the war.

explicit_text: We lived aboard the APL-14, which was a Navy barge converted into a floating barracks for about 600 of us. It was like a hotel on the water but very unstable, especially during storms. I slept on the motor deck, and the whole thing would roll so much that gear would fall on people below.

explicit_text: Ulithi was the largest natural harbor in the world and a critical supply base during the war. We supplied ammunition to the fleets there, moving about a hundred tons a night. It was also the site of a Japanese mini-submarine attack, which caused a tanker to catch fire and led to several days of intense anti-submarine activity.

explicit_text: At Ulithi, we used Higgins boats to ferry supplies and personnel to larger ships. These wooden landing craft had a front door that would drop down for beach landings. They were essential for moving men and materials between ships and shore during the war.

explicit_text: We also used Landing Craft Mechanized, or LCMs, which were steel-bodied boats used to transport tanks and heavy equipment. They had a coxswain to pilot them and were vital for moving large loads to ships like the USS New Jersey. These crafts were part of the logistics chain supporting the fleet.

explicit_text: We loaded ammunition onto the USS New Jersey, a battleship, including sixteen-inch shells and powder, as well as smaller caliber rounds for anti-aircraft guns. This was part of the preparation for General Halsey's planned attack on Japan. Working on such a powerful ship was impressive and critical to the war effort.

explicit_text: During our time at Ulithi, the Japanese launched a surprise attack using six one-man mini-submarines. They managed to blow up an oil tanker, causing a massive fire. Our destroyers spent three days dropping depth charges to hunt these subs, creating a tense and exhausting period for everyone on the base.
